coredata.dc:title: Aptasensors for quantitative detection of Salmonella Typhimurium
coredata.dc:creator: Ansari, Najmeh
Yazdian-Robati, Rezvan
Shahdordizadeh, Mahin
Wang, Zhouping
Ghazvini, Kiarash
coredata.dc:description: Salmonella is one of the most frequent causes of food borne infectious disease. Among nearly 2500 documented serotypes are reported, Salmonella Typhimurium is the number one serotype associated with salmonellosis worldwide. Many different methods have been developed for the detection and quantification of S. typhimurium. Most of these assays are usually expensive, time consuming and require difficult sample preparation steps. Therefore, it is necessary to develop rapid, robust, cost-effective and sensitive alternative detection methods. In the last years, aptasensors, used for detection of S. typhimurium in different samples. In this review, recent advances and applications of aptasensors for the detection and quantification of S. typhimurium in details have been summarized.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Food borne infectious
Salmonella Typhimurium
Detection
Aptasensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical aptasensor for S. typhimurium detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of aptasensor based on aptamer-AuNPs and silver enhancement for the detection of S. typhimurium. Biotinylated aptamer 1 was immobilized with streptavidin through the binding of biotin and streptavidin onto the surface of microtiter plate-wells. Then, Salmonella Typhimurium and aptamer 2-AuNPs conjugates were incubated to form sandwich-type aptamer/bacteria/aptamer-AuNPs complexes. Adapted from the published works of [85].

Schematic illustration of colorimetric aptasensor for S. typhimurium detection using aptamer modified AuNPs and MNPs. Thiol aptamer and biotin aptamer immobilized on the surface of AuNPs and MNPs respectively. The formed complexes were separated which lead to fading of AuNPs suspension and a decrease of UV/Vis signal. Adapted from the published works of [28].

Scheme of the colorimetric ELAAS, chemiluminescent ELAAS or nano-ELAAS for the detection of S. typhimurium. (A) Preparation of nanoprobes. (B) Preparation of MMP-aptamers. (C) Comparison of the three signal amplification methods. Adapted from the published works of [82].

Schematic of the MNP-based colorimetric aptasensor, using label-free DNA aptamers and TMB. In the presence of target aptamer detached from the MNPs and the peroxidase activity of MNPs recovered causing a blue-colored product. Adapted from the published works of ( [56]. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

two colors green and red QDs and C-NPs
aptamer conjugated carbon quantum dots complexes
nanogold labeling and silver signal amplification

head.ce:title: Aptasensors for quantitative detection of Salmonella Typhimurium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology and Virology, Faculty of Medicine, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Biotechnology, School of Pharmacy, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology
School of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Antimicrobial Resistance Research Center, Buali Research Institute, Department of Microbiology and Virology, Faculty of Medicine, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Salmonella is one of the most frequent causes of food borne infectious disease. Among nearly 2500 documented serotypes are reported, Salmonella Typhimurium is the number one serotype associated with salmonellosis worldwide. Many different methods have been developed for the detection and quantification of S. typhimurium. Most of these assays are usually expensive, time consuming and require difficult sample preparation steps. Therefore, it is necessary to develop rapid, robust, cost-effective and sensitive alternative detection methods. In the last years, aptasensors, used for detection of S. typhimurium in different samples. In this review, recent advances and applications of aptasensors for the detection and quantification of S. typhimurium in details have been summarized.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection is responsible of many serious and fatal diseases. The diagnosis of pathogenic bacteria is an essential urge of the clinical microbiology. Among all the food-borne pathogens, Salmonella is one of the most frequent causes of food borne infectious disease, especially gastroenteritis [50]. Nearly 2500 documented serotypes are reported, Salmonella Typhimurium is the number one serotype associated with salmonellosis worldwide [34]. S. typhimurium, a Gram-negative bacteria pathogen, transmitted primarily through the consumption of raw or uncooked eggs, vegetables, fruits, and poultry [14,52] and causes of food-borne illness in human and animal hosts worldwide [7]. The presence of this organism in food can be a serious threat to public health. Also lipopolysaccharides (LPS) as endotoxins are integral components of the outer membrane of all Gram-negative bacteria such as S. typhimurium. LPS leads to septic shock [15], pyrogenic reaction [54], hypotension, diarrhea and vascular blood clotting [42], therefore detection of LPS is also crucial for medical and food security. So sensitive and quantification methods for S. typhimurium detection are critical in food quality and security. The routine detection methods for S. typhimurium are culture-based methods, amplification-based assays, real-time polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [57,68,87] and immunoassays such as enzyme-linked assays, chemiluminescent immunoassays, and fluorescence immunoassay [13,49,83,84]. These methods have some limitations such as requiring specialized instrumentation and highly trained personnel as well as some methods are time consuming with false negative results. Therefore, there is a continuous need for a rapid, sensitive and reliable assay for S. typhimurium detection. Biosensors are analytical devices with two main functional components, target recognition and signal transduction, which can sense and quantity targets by changing the recognition of targets into a physically detectable signal, such as electronic, magnetic or optical signals. Aptamer is single-stranded nucleic acid (DNA or RNA) with high affinity and a significant conformation upon binding with a wide range of targets. Typically, aptamer sequences are selected through an in vitro Systematic Evolution of Ligands by EXponential enrichment (SELEX) [11]. Aptamer-based biosensors, known as aptasensors, have been developed as potential methods for bacterial recognition. Furthermore, aptamers can be easily improved with different markers in order to either increase their stability or labeling them with fluorophores as well as quenchers to facilitate the production of biosensors [12]. Aptasensors, facilitate detection of pathogens without any need to tedious procedures such as multiple incubations and washings [43]. Up till now, various aptamers have been identified which targeted many bacteria, such as Staphylococcus aureus [9], Lactobacillus acidophilus [36], Vibrio parahaemolyticus [21], Salmonella Typhimurium ( [22], Campylobacter jejuni [30], Group A Streptococcus [35], Listeria monocytogenes [19], Mycobacterium tuberculosis [10] and Shigella dysenteriae [20]. Aptamers can potentially be functional to diagnosis of pathogenic bacteria because of specificity and high affinity to their target, rapid and reproducible synthesis, and convenient modifications [25]. In this review, we will focus on recent advances in aptamer based biosensors developed for S. typhimurium as the most important serotype of this genus.

Optical aptasensors are the combination of aptamer as the recognition part with different optical analytical techniques as the signal transductions. Optical analysis methods are extensively used in the constructions of aptasensors due to their high sensitivity, rapid response and simple use. There are five main classes of optical aptasensores including colorimetry, chemiluminescence (CL), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), surface plasmon resonance (SPR), and fluorescence [33,63]. In this section, we explained all optical aptasensors for S. typhimurium based on detection techniques such as SERS, fluorescence and colorimetry.

Application of SERS has been reported in many different fields, such as the environmental monitoring [6,53], biomedical field [37,41], and food quality assurance [1,48]. Also some researchers used SERS-based assays to detection of bacterial cells [5,73]. There have been some reports of using a SERS sensing platform based on an aptamer for the sensitive and rapid detection of S. typhimurium. In one method the Au@Ag core/shell NPs exploited as the enhanced substrate for SERS and apt1 was immobilized on the surface of Au@Ag core/shellNPs. ROX-modified apt2 attached with the target in the same way as Au@Ag-apt1 and the Raman signal intensity was generated from it [18]. In the presence of the S. typhimurium, Au@Ag-apt1 recognized and attached to S. typhimurium. Following the addition of ROX-modified apt2, Au@Ag-apt 1-target-apt 2-ROX sandwich-like complexes organized based on the high specificity and affinity of aptamer and S. typhimurium. More S. typhimurium was added, the surface loading of bound ROX-apt 2 increased, resulting in increased SERS intensity. By monitoring the SERS signal increase along with changes in concentration, highly sensitive quantification of S. typhimurium could be realized. The linear relationship between the SERS intensity and the S. typhimurium concentration was in the range from 15 to 1.5 × 106 cfu/ml. The detection limit was found to be 15 cfu/mL the linear regression equation is defined as Y = 592.54x−768.98, and the corresponding correlation coefficient was 0.996. This technique can potentially be used for the rapid and sensitive detection of other pathogenic bacteria for food safety assurance. Common SERS-enhanced substrates are silver nanoparticles, gold nanoparticles and other heavy metal nanomaterials [8,38,60]. A SERS-aptasensor, using nanoparticles was established for the quantitative detection of S. typhimurium and S. aureus simultaneously with the detection limit of 35 cfu/mL for S. aureus and 15 cfu/mL for S. typhimurium by Zhang and coworkers [86]. In this platform, gold nanoparticles (GNPs) modified with Raman molecules (Mercaptobenzoic acid (MBA) and 5, 5′-Dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DNTP)) and aptamers were applied as the signal probe. Thiolated aptamers of S. typhimurium and S. aureus are as the capture probe with MBA and DNTP respectively, fixed on Fe3O4 magnetic gold nanoparticles (MGNPs). In this platform the Raman intensified spectrum demonstrated the quantification of S. typhimurium and S. aureus.

Among aptamer-based assays, fluorescence detection methods are attractive, to some extent because of their high sensitivity, feasibility of quantification and potential for high-throughput analysis [51]. Labeled and label-free fluorescence aptasensors are two main approaches for these aptasensors [33]. Most current fluorescent aptamer-based methods require fluorophore or fluorescent nanoparticle labeling of the aptamer molecules [2,45,46]. These labeled fluorescent approaches are laborious, time-consuming and expensive. Therefore, it is essential to develop convenient and label-free approaches for pathogenic bacteria detection. The fluorescence of PicoGreen, SYBR Green dye and AccuBlue reagent themselves are very weak; however, the dye molecule becomes highly fluorescent on binding to dsDNA, but when it binds to ssDNA no considerable fluorescence change can be observed. A universal fluorescent aptasensor with two models, ‘‘signal on’’ and ‘‘signal off,’’ based on AccuBlue dye with unmodified aptamers to detect S. typhimurium was introduced by Duan et al. [25]. In the presence of target, aptamer bound to its target, causing the cDNA to separate from the dsDNA duplex and leading to low fluorescence intensity as a result of the release of AccuBlue. The intercalation time of AccuBlue into the aptamer/cDNA duplex distinctly influenced the fluoresence signal. This aptamer based sensor showed a linear range from 50 to 106 cfu/mL with a low LOD of 25 cfu/mL. Benefitting AccuBlue and elimination of DNA labeling this sensor can be adapted for detection of wide variety of other pathogens [25].

Quantum dots (QDs), as efficient fluorescence nanomaterials and flow cytometry, as a high throughput biophysical analytical technique were served for quantitative detection of Vibrio parahaemolyticus and S. typhimurium. In this DNA aptasensor quantum dots (QDs) as a fluorescence marker and aptamers as the molecular recognition element and fluorescence carrier used to detect these pathogenic bacteria with the detection limit of 5 × 103 cfu/mL. In this sensor two different QDs with emission at 535 and the other at 585 nm employed as optical labels. Specific aptamers for V. parahaemolyticus and S. typhimurium attached covalently to the QDs. In the presence of two pathogenic bacteria in the sample, aptamers specifically bound to them and the respective emission signals related to the number of cells detect [26]. Also this group, prepared a novel aptasensor for the simultaneous determination of S. typhimurium based on a FRET (Fluorescence resonance energy transfer) [24].

In this FRET system, two colors green and red QDs were utilized as energy donors and carbon nanoparticles (C –NPs) with high energy absorption efficiency as acceptors. The fluorescence of QDs is strongly quenched in the presence of CNPs. Upon recognition of the target bacteria by QDs-aptamer, its binding affinity to CNPs became weak and fluorescence intensity was measured as a function of S. typhimurium concentration. This sensor showed an ultralow detection limit as 35 cfu/mL, for pathogenic bacteria in food samples [24].

The fluorescent carbon dot (CDs) as a novel class of nanocarbons, has been the focus of much recent attention because of excellent properties including high fluorescence quantum yield, easy preparation, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and superior fluorescence properties [16,59]. By taking advantage of these novel labels, Wang and co-workers designed an aptasensor based on aptamer-conjugated CDs for the rapid and quantitative detection of S. typhimurium with a LOD of 50 cfu/mL in solutions of eggshell and tap water [78]. In this work, CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using the citric acid as carbon source and in order to form CDs - aptamer the modified CDs with carboxyl attached to amino groups of specific aptamers. In the presence of bacteria fluorescence intensity of CDs enhanced and a linear relation between the fluorescence intensity and concentrations of S. typhimurium in the range of 103–105 cfu/mL was detected [78].

Graphene oxide (GO), a two-dimensional (2D)-layered precursor of graphene preparation [64], consists of sp2-bonded carbon with a hexagonal configuration. Extraordinary physical properties of graphene, including excellent electrical conductivity, high mechanical strength, very-large specific surface area, biocompatibility and tunable optical properties are due to these bonds and electron configuration. GO as an exceptional energy acceptor in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is an ideal material for the production of biosensor and GO is functional in fluorescence quenching detection schemes [29].

Based on these mentioned excellent properties of GO, an GO aptasensor was introduced as a platform for the sensitive and selective detection of S. typhimurium, in which one end was labeled with the fluorophore5-Carboxyfluorescein (FAM), as the energy donor and GO as the energy acceptor [29]. FAM-aptamer binds to the GO by π-π stacking interaction and because of FRET, fluorescence quenching of FAM takes place. The addition of target causes the formation of FAM-aptamer/target complexes, which makes FAM-aptamer keep away from the GO and leads to the restoration of fluorescence. This assay exhibit a linear response to bacteria in the concentration range from 1 × 103 to 1 × 108 CFU/mL, with a detection limit of 100 CFU/mL [25].

Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) as fluorescent biolabels, with several attractive properties such as significant optical and chemical characteristics long lifetimes, high resistance to photobleaching and photochemical degradation provided a new field for the construction of aptasensors [65,76,80]. Moreover comparison to the common organic fluorophores and QDs, biological samples cannot absorb the excitation light of UCNP [47]. Lanthanide-doped near-infrared (NIR) to visible (UCNPs) are able to emit strong visible luminescence with the excitation of NIR light (typically 980 nm). Three types of UCNPs that emit the light at different wavelengths were decorated with food pathogen specific aptamers and utilized for specific and rapid detection of S. typhimurium, S. aureus, and V. parahemolyticus [80]. In This proposed method, food matrix did not require multiple pretreatment processes due to the use of magnetic nanoparticles. The linear ranges for the three food pathogenic bacteria were 50 to 106 cfu/mL, and the limit of detection (LOD) for S. typhimurium was found to be 15 cfu/mL [80].

Another Multicolor UCNPs were applied as labels for the detection of S. typhimurium and S. aureus simultaneously with the limits of detection 5 and 8 cfu/mL respectively [27]. Recently with the advance in nanotechnology, fluorescent nanomaterials play an increasingly important role in biosensors [32,62,74]. By considering the extraordinary properties of aptamer and fluorescent nanomaterials for target labeling and signaling, Wang et al. fabricated a biosensor for detection of food-borne pathogen [77]. In this aptasensor, biotin-labeled DNA aptamer and streptavidin-conjugated silica nanoparticles used as the detection probe for detection of S. typhimurium. In samples with bacterial contamination, under the fluorescence microscope, red fluorescence were obviously distinguished. In contrast, there was no obvious fluorescence image in the control group, indicating the control group samples are bacteria S. typhimurium free.

Colorimetry assay as the simplest sensing method has been used extensively in design of aptasensors [70]. Gold and magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) owing to their size/distance-dependent, the presence of various binding domains and optical properties have received much attention for using in colorimetric biosensors [33,55]. Based on the phenomenon that the appearance color of dispersion state is different from that in aggregation state several optical aptasensors using gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been designed [31,63]. Wu et al. reported an AuNPs-based aptasensors for the highly selective detection of S. typhimurium and E. coli O157:H7 [81]. In the presence of target, aptamer bound to its target and upon addition of high-salt solution aggregation of AuNPs occurs, leading to the color change from red to purple/blue. The detection limit was 105 CFU/mL, which was significantly lower [61] than or comparable [79] to the currently available antibody-based biosensors with the same bacteria. In order to detect lower levels of bacteria, sample preconcentration steps, such as microfiltration and immunomagnetic separation will be necessary prior to analysis. In addition, this method does not need the labeling of aptamers or modification of AuNPs and specialized instrumentation, which significantly reduce the cost. A silver reduction based colorimetric sensing method and nanogold labeling was proposed by Yuan and colleagues [85]. This assay depends on catalytic activity of AuNP not its aggregation.

In this biosensor S. typhimurium specific aptamer as a recognition element for bacterial cells immobilized to microtitre plate wells by avidin-biotin linkage. At the same time, a second aptamers were conjugated to AuNPs by thiol were incubated in the wells to form sandwich-type aptamer/bacteria/aptamer-AuNPs complexes. Then a silver enhancer solution was utilized to enhance the signals and the cells could be quantified at sensitive levels with naked eye)Fig. 1 ). The detection limit of the proposed method reached 7 cfu/mL [85].

Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) possess distinguished characteristics such as reproducibility, cost-effectiveness and uniformity in size and ease of use for separation and good biocompatibility [23]. According mentioned properties, this nanoparticles employed in different field including immunoassays, DNA hybridization analysis, protein and enzyme immobilization, cell separation and drug delivery [39,69,75]. Based on excellent separation and concentration ability of MNPs and high extinction coefficient of AuNPs, a simple and fast colorimetric method using AuNPs as an indicator and MNPs as a concentration elements have been reported [28]. In this biosensing method, thiol aptamer and biotin aptamer immobilized on the surface of AuNPs and MNPs respectively (Fig. 2 ). In the absence of S. typhimurium, the AuNPs-thiol apt and MNPs-biotin apt were separated in solution, and red color appeared. In the presence of a target, due to the high affinity of aptamer to the corresponding target, S. typhimurium were captured by AuNPs-apt1 and MNPs-apt2, then concentrated by the external magnetic field resulting in fading in the color of AuNPs suspension and a reduce in the UV-V absorbance. This aptasensor shows a linear range from 25 to 105 cfu/mL and a detection limit of 10 cfu/mL [28].

Wu et al. fabricated a sophisticated gold nanoparticle-based enzyme-linked antibody-aptamer sandwich (Nano-ELAAS) for the rapid, sensitive and quantitative detection of S. typhimurium with LOD of 1 × 103 CFU/mL [82]. In this platform, MNP were modified with S. typhimurium aptamers to act as the capture probes. MNP-aptamers and detection antibodies with S. typhimurium created a sandwich form, which were collected by a magnet. Then three signal reporters (HRP-labeled reporter antibody, AP labeled reporter antibody and double codified nanoprobe) bound to this complex and developing colorimetric ELAAS, chemiluminescent ELAAS and nano-ELAAS, respectively (Fig. 3 ). Compare with colorimetric and chemiluminescent assay, nano-ELAAS had a low detection limit and high specificity to S. typhimurium.

Aptamer functionalized magnetic bead (AFMB) are very promising in extraction and enrichment of S. typhimurium from complex biological samples. Du et al. designed a sensor though suspension polymerization and grafted with glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) brushes. Then S. typhimurium aptamer were fixed on the surface of beads using glutaraldehyde linking. The numbers of captured bacteria cells were quantified by RT PCR. The LOD of this biosensor can be found to be as 78 CFU/mL in milk samples [17]. Based on the reported LODs, among the different optical methods, the fluorescence aptasensors had the highest sensitivity (5-5 × 105 CFU/mL) and colorimetric aptasensors was reported to have the lowest sensitivity (7.5 × 105 CFU/mL). A summary of the reports on S. typhimurium aptasensors based on optical methods are provided in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical aptasensor for S. typhimurium detection
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical biosensors are widely used for the detection and quantification of food-borne pathogens because of unique properties such as robust, simple operation, high sensitivity and portability [58]. Also electrochemical methods require less amount of analyte for detection and are label free so decreases cost and LOD of aptasensor [66]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a powerful analysis technique to detect the changes of the interfacial properties of the electrode after the interaction of analytes with their probing molecules immobilized on electrode surfaces. EIS provides a rapid, sensitive and nondestructive platform based on probing impedance variations along reaction, electrode/electrolyte, and interfaces [71]. An EIS aptasensor with the ability to detection of LPS has been successfully constructed by Su and colleagues to detect bacterial endotoxin or LPS [72]. In the designed platform amine-terminated aptamer ssDNA (Kd = 11.9 nM) was immobilized on a gold electrode via 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) as a linker. The developed aptasensor showed a good linear relationship between the changes in the charge-transfer resistance ( Δ Ret) and the logarithmic value of LPS concentration was in a broad dynamic detection range of 0.001–1 ng/mL [72]. This group reported similar result with a detection limit of 0.005 ng/mL [71]. Recently Labia's team have been developed the first aptamer-based viability impedimetric sensor for detection of viable S. typhimurium (AptaVISens-B) from other Salmonella species, including S. enteritidis and S. choleraesuis [44]. In this sensor highly specific DNA aptamers to live S. typhimurium was integrated into an impedimetric sensor via self-assembly onto a gold nanoparticle-modified screen-printed carbon electrode (GNP-SPCE). The presented aptasensor is highly selective and can be distinguished between live and killed bacteria with the LOD of 600 CFU/mL (equivalent to 18 live cells in 30 μL of assay volume) [44]. Another sensitive and rapid impedimetric aptasensor for detection of Salmonella has been developed based on the one-step electrode position of nanocomposites on surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) [40]. The nanocomposite consisting of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and carboxy modified multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) worked as both a signal amplification system and an aptamer-loaded system. An amino modified aptamer specific for Salmonella bound to the rGO-MWCNT composite through amide bonds. In presence Salmonella, electron transfer is blocked by the combination of aptamer and its target. Hence Salmonella can be easily determined by increase in the resistance value with a detection limit of 25 cfu/mL [40].

Electrochemical immobilization of the diazonium-grafting layer allowed the formation of a denser aptamer layer, which resulted in higher sensitivity for S. typhimurium with a LOD of 6 cfu/mL [4]. In this aptasensor, following modification screen printed carbon electrodes (SPEs) with the diazonium-grafting layer, aminated-aptamer immobilized on the activated surface. The developed aptamer-biosensor showed a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 1 × 101 CFU/mL [4]. Conductive conjugated polymers (CP) are very attractive and flexible materials for the construction of functional interfaces and sensing surfaces [3]. Among CPs, Polypyrrole-based polymers have been widely used for the fabrication of chemo/biosensors and functional surfaces, due to their intrinsic chemical and electrical properties. Sheikhzadeh group have been reported the development of a label-free impedimetric electrochemical biosensor for the detection of S. typhimurium based on the combination of poly [pyrrole-co-3-carboxyl-pyrrole] copolymer and aptamer as sensing surface without the need of the addition of any reporting redox probe following aptamer pathogen interaction [67]. The proposed method can be detect S. typhimurium with high selectivity with LOQ of 100 CFU/mL and a LOD of 3 CFU/mL. Available electrochemical-based S. typhimurium aptasensors have been summarized in Table 2 .

In conclusion, in this review, we presented the developments of aptamer-based biosensors for S. typhimurium detection based on optical an electrochemical analysis approaches. There is great interest in utilizing of aptasensors for detection of food contamination. Aptamers are the best selection for designing of point-of-care pathogen testing to identify trace levels of bacteria which could not be measured by ordinary methods. Among optical aptasensors, fluorescence and colorimetric are the most prevalent methods used for the detection of S. typhimurium sensors, because of its great characteristics such as high sensitivity and simple detection procedure. Among different optical assays, UCNPS as fluorescent biolabels in fluorescence aptasensor, showed the highest sensitivity for S. typhimurium (5 cfu/mL) in comparison with other optical aptasensors. Calorimetric method is the simplest technique which can easily differentiate the presence of target by naked eye. Application of nanogold and silver nanoparticle in the construction of colorimetric aptasensor decreased the LOD. Based on the reported LODs, label-free impedimetric electrochemical biosensor have the highest sensitivity and lowest LODs among all developed aptasensors 3 CFU/mL [67]. The present article, the art of biosensors-based aptamers showed some limitation such as the lack of some instruments, materials and more charges in some strategies. Also specificity and function in some mentioned aptasensors depend on the sample conditions such as pH, temperature, viscosity, and ionic strength. Furthermore, aptamers sometimes intract with the sample matrix nonspecifically and lead to false or imprecise results. To overcome these problems, scientists are trying to increase the benefits of aptasensors by improvement of the available procedures. Future studies on developing aptasensors for S. typhimurium may help to combat with the food borne epidemics with soon detection and monitoring.
The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest and each author share similar contribution to this article.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) applied to cancer diagnosis and detection of pesticides, explosives, and drugs

Combining use of a panel of ssDNA aptamers in the detection of Staphylococcus aureus

A new method for detection of endotoxin on polymyxin B-immobilized gold electrodes

Fluorescent aptasensor for the determination of Salmonella Typhimurium based on a graphene oxide platform

DNA aptamers binding to multiple prevalent M-types of Streptococcus pyogenes
Selection of aptamers against live bacterial cells

Au–Ag bimetallic nanodendrite synthesized via simultaneous co-electrodeposition and its application as a SERS substrate
